quote: Is there anybody else on the title of the home with you?
stewart_read: Clean execution. The rep asked the title question directly and got a clear 'No. It would just be me.' This eliminated the spouse-coordination obstacle entirely — no protocol.spouse_decision complication, no co-decider to schedule around. Worth noting because it makes the missed booking that follows more notable: the rep had a solo-decision-maker, qualified lead, and still didn't close an appointment.
coaching_implication: Use this as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1. The rep did this right. Then pivot: 'You cleared the hardest structural obstacle on this call — no spouse to coordinate. So what stopped you from booking the appointment right here?'

quote: I think I just clicked on a link or something about it, and then out of nowhere, I started getting calls.
stewart_read: This looks like a low-motivation signal but it is actually a curiosity-creation opening. Mike has never looked at solar, has a pool, owns Airbnbs, and is paying ~$300/month on a single-occupant home — meaning his load is likely to grow. The accidental-lead disclosure is the exact moment the schema's interest_in_solar depth probe is designed for: 'What would it mean for you if you could cut that bill down, given everything you've got going on with the properties?' The rep instead validated the annoyance ('That's kinda annoying. Sorry about that.') and moved on.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was strategically de-escalating call-fatigue before pivoting to curiosity creation. Rejected: the rep never pivoted. The next move was utility company, not a depth probe.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the schema note: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Then ask: 'What could you have said here that would have made Mike curious about his own numbers?' The pool and the Airbnbs are the leverage — the rep walked past both.

quote: Yeah. A little bit, I guess.
stewart_read: This is the second touch on the accidental-lead pattern. Mike's 'a little bit, I guess' is the lowest-energy interest signal possible. The rep accepted it and moved to utility company. The schema's objection_inversion framework had a move available here: flip the low-interest framing toward the bill ('What you're not interested in is staying with what you have') or anchor to the pool load ('With a pool running, your bill is probably higher in summer — have you ever looked at what that's actually costing you?'). Neither was attempted.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was correctly reading Mike as a low-priority lead and conserving energy. Rejected: Mike is a solo-title homeowner, good credit, first-time solar, pool, Airbnbs — this is a high-value lead regardless of his stated interest level. Low stated interest on an accidental lead is the norm, not a DQ signal.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 06:01 moment in the 1-on-1. The rep had two consecutive opportunities to create curiosity and took neither. Walk through coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion: the flip doesn't have to be logically airtight — it's a pattern interrupt. Practice the move out loud.

quote: No. That's awesome. I actually so I just barely finished real estate school. I've been wanting to get into that kind of stuff.
stewart_read: Mike's real estate and Airbnb disclosure at 03:10 was a genuine engagement spike — the highest-energy moment in the call. The rep matched his energy but redirected it entirely toward her own career aspirations rather than back into qualification. The failure here is not setter_scope_discipline (no solar-technical content was discussed) — it is a failure to re-enter the verify sequence after a customer-initiated off-topic pull. The rep had a natural bridge available: 'That's impressive — with multiple properties and Airbnbs, your energy costs must add up. Is the solar interest for this home specifically, or are you thinking about it across the properties?' Instead, the call spent 90 seconds on Utah real estate.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately building rapport to warm up a cold lead before pivoting to qualification. Rejected: the rep never pivoted back. The verify sequence resumed at 05:09 with roof type, skipping any depth on interest_in_solar entirely.
coaching_implication: The rep's instinct to match Mike's energy was correct. The error was not having a bridge back. In the 1-on-1, ask: 'After he told you about the Airbnbs, what was the next question you should have asked about solar?' The answer is in verify.interest_in_solar — 'What interested you in solar?' with depth. The Airbnbs are the hook.

quote: Well, it would mainly well, I got a I got a couple thing. I own couple real estate, you know, rent renting and all that. I own a couple Airbnbs.
stewart_read: Multiple Airbnbs and rental properties signal a sophisticated, financially active customer who thinks in terms of ROI and asset management. This is not a fixed-income retiree skeptical of a new expense — this is someone who already understands the logic of investing to reduce ongoing costs. The rep had a high-leverage framing available: solar as an asset play, not a utility bill reduction. That framing would have landed differently with this customer than the standard 'we can cut that down for you a little bit' at 07:02. The rep walked past the signal entirely.
coaching_implication: Flag this for the manager as a missed qualification depth moment. The Airbnb/real estate profile changes the pitch framing — this customer responds to ROI language, not bill-pain language. The rep should have noted this and used it. Ask in the 1-on-1: 'How would you pitch solar differently to someone who owns Airbnbs versus someone on a fixed income?'

quote: you have the pool the pool and the basketball court?
stewart_read: The rep spotted the pool on the satellite image and called it out — but treated it as a rapport moment ('That's a beautiful home') rather than an energy-load signal. A pool is a schema-level enthusiasm signal: higher electric load, higher bill, stronger solar ROI case. The rep had the information and did not use it. Mike's $300/month bill on a single-occupant home with a pool is almost certainly understated or seasonal — the pool load in summer would spike that number. The rep accepted $300 flat and said 'we can still cut that down for you a little bit' at 07:02.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the schema enthusiasm_signal category for pools. The pool was visible on the satellite image — the rep saw it and complimented the home instead of anchoring it to bill pain. In the 1-on-1: 'When you saw the pool, what should your next question have been?' Expected answer: 'What does your bill look like in the summer when the pool is running?' That question opens the real bill conversation.

quote: Yeah. What we can do is I can just send you a text from my personal phone, and then just whenever you get home and and have access to one of those, if you could just send me a photo of it, then that'd be great.
stewart_read: The rep conflated bill collection with appointment booking and treated 'I'm not home' as a reason not to book. These are two separate actions. The bill is needed before the sit (if paid lead) or preferred but not required (if organic lead) — but the appointment can and should be booked independently of bill availability. The rep should have booked the appointment first, then set the bill-collection follow-up as a pre-sit task. Instead, the call ended with a personal-phone text promise and no locked time. Additionally, the personal-phone follow-up is flagged in the schema as an anti-pattern: privacy exposure, no systematic follow-up, no attribution trail.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was correctly following paid-lead protocol requiring bill before appointment. Cannot confirm or rule out paid-lead protocol — lead type is unknown to Stewart per call. Regardless of lead type, the correct sequence is: book appointment first, collect bill as pre-sit task. If paid lead: bill is required before the sit, not before the booking. If organic lead: bill wasn't required at all. The personal-phone follow-up is an anti-pattern under either scenario.
coaching_implication: This is the call's most consequential miss. Walk the rep through the sequence: book first, bill second. Then flag the personal-phone anti-pattern per bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture. Ask: 'What would you have said to book the appointment before asking for the bill?' The rep needs a script for 'I'm not home right now' that doesn't end the call.

quote: About 300 a month? K. Yeah. Yeah. No problem. We can still cut that down for you a little bit.
stewart_read: The rep captured the bill number and immediately minimized it ('a little bit'). This is the schema's failure pattern verbatim: 'ask + acknowledge + move on' instead of 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' The $300 number was not flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. With a pool on the property and Airbnbs in the portfolio, the rep had multiple angles to make $300 feel like the floor, not the ceiling. 'A little bit' undersells the program and undersells the customer's own pain.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately understating savings to avoid overpromising on a first-time solar customer. Rejected: the schema's objection_inversion framework does not require fabricated numbers — the flip is emotional, not numerical. 'What you're paying now is the expense solar replaces' requires no invented savings figure.
coaching_implication: This is a universal floor coaching priority per the schema: 'Across all 7 deep-read calls, every rep captured the bill anchor and NONE flipped it.' Use this moment to introduce the flip framework. Ask the rep: 'What could you have said after he told you $300 that would have made him feel something about that number?' Then practice the flip out loud.
